SOURCE 3: KAPUNGU RUHERESHWA - TEACHER TO ADVOCATE (9/10)

URL: https://tsosrefugees.org/stories/from-refugee-to-advocate-for-children
PUBLICATION: Their Story Is Our Story (TSOS) - June 12, 2025
INTERVIEWED BY: Katie Willis

==================================================================================
FULL NARRATIVE ARC
==================================================================================

Born Democratic Republic of Congo 1965 → married, 4 children (1 son, 3 daughters) → finished college 1987 → high school teacher 15 years → "after some incidents in my country" → opened OPR (Organization, Peace, and Reconciliation) "for the cause of bringing people together" → war started → had to leave country, become refugee in Tanzania → continued OPR organization in refugee camp → joined group teaching communication with deaf/mute people → "This was a good opportunity for me" → spent 19 YEARS in refugee camp using sign language to communicate with those who couldn't talk or hear → 2015: Tanzania government said not safe to return to DRC → accepted to live in United States with family → "My family and I were very happy about this" → arrived in America → "kept thinking about the kids in the refugee camp who were suffering–no clothes, no shoes, no school supplies" → opened organization ADEVS (Aid to Vulnerable School Children) → TWO OBJECTIVES: (1) Change life of kids in bad situations in Africa + assist refugees/children in America adapt to new life, partnership with Refuge Bowling Green (clothes, shoes, uniforms to Nyarugusu Camp Tanzania + Nakivale Camp Uganda), help with transportation in USA (ESL classes, grocery shopping, job interviews), helping with sports; (2) Educate about drugs/alcohol/child abuse both Africa and America, educate newcomers about laws, help people change (violence/discipline methods) → "I am grateful that people can read my story and see where to help"

==================================================================================
KEY EMOTIONAL MOMENTS (18+)
==================================================================================

1. College degree 1987 (educational achievement)
2. High school teacher 15 years (career established)
3. "After some incidents in my country" (understatement of trauma)
4. Founded OPR for peace and reconciliation (proactive peacemaking)
5. "War started" (life disrupted)
6. Had to leave country, become refugee
7. Continued OPR in Tanzania camp (persistence of mission)
8. Joined group teaching deaf/mute communication
9. "This was a good opportunity for me" (finding purpose in adversity)
10. 19 YEARS in refugee camp (extraordinary duration)
11. Sign language as communication method (skill development)
12. 2015: Not safe to return (permanence of displacement)
13. Accepted to USA "My family and I were very happy about this"
14. Arrival in America
15. "Kept thinking about the kids in the refugee camp who were suffering"
16. Couldn't stop thinking about children (moral imperative)
17. Founded ADEVS (action from compassion)
18. Partnership with Refuge Bowling Green (community collaboration)
19. Transportation help in USA (practical service)
20. Two camps in Africa receiving aid (Nyarugusu, Nakivale)
21. Educating about drugs/alcohol/child abuse (prevention)
22. Helping people change discipline methods (cultural bridge)
23. "I am grateful that people can read my story and see where to help"

==================================================================================
DIRECT QUOTES
==================================================================================

"I finished college in 1987 in my country, and I was a high school teacher for 15 years."

"After some incidents in my country, I opened an organization called OPR: Organization, Peace, and Reconciliation, for the cause of bringing people together."

"This was a good opportunity for me. I spent 19 years in this refugee camp, using sign language to communicate with those people who couldn't talk or hear."

"In 2015, after 19 years in the refugee camp, the Tanzania government said it was not safe to go back to my country, and told me I was accepted to go live in the United States with my family. My family and I were very happy about this."

"When I got to America, I kept thinking about the kids in the refugee camp who were suffering–no clothes, no shoes, no school supplies."

"Most projects focus so much on the learners—but no one ever thinks about the teachers."

"I am grateful that people can read my story and see where to help."

==================================================================================
ESL MULTIPLIERS (6/6)
==================================================================================

✓ BUILDING NEW LIFE: DRC teacher → refugee → sign language teacher → USA resident → ADEVS founder → serving two continents
✓ BELONGING: Teaching community DRC, OPR members, refugee camp community (19 years), deaf/mute community, USA community, Refuge Bowling Green partnership, children in Africa camps
✓ CONNECTION ACROSS DIFFERENCES: Teacher → refugees, hearing → deaf/mute, refugee → resettled, African → American, cultural discipline methods bridge
✓ OVERCOMING "NOT GOOD ENOUGH": Displaced teacher → found purpose in camp → learned sign language → founded international organization → helping both continents
✓ PERSISTENCE: 15 years teaching → war → continued OPR in camp → 19 years in camp → resettled → immediately founded new org → sustained service
✓ FIRST-TIME COURAGE: First time founding OPR, first time fleeing, first time in refugee camp, first time learning sign language, first time in USA, first time founding ADEVS

==================================================================================
TRANSFORMATION ANALYSIS
==================================================================================

BEFORE: College educated (1987), high school teacher (15 years), DRC, established career
DURING CONFLICT: Founded OPR (peace organization), war started, forced to flee
DURING CAMP: Tanzania refugee camp, continued OPR, learned sign language, taught deaf/mute, 19 YEARS
DURING TRANSITION: 2015 not safe to return, accepted to USA, family happy
AFTER USA: Kept thinking about suffering children, founded ADEVS, two objectives (Africa support + USA adaptation), partnership Refuge Bowling Green, transportation help, education about drugs/alcohol/abuse, helping change discipline methods
IMPACT: Serving children in two camps (Nyarugusu Tanzania, Nakivale Uganda) + refugees in USA

==================================================================================
RATING: 9/10
==================================================================================

STRENGTHS: Full circle (teacher → refugee → advocate for children), 19 years in camp (extraordinary), sign language skill, dual continent service (Africa + USA), practical help (clothes, shoes, transportation), cultural bridge (discipline methods), peace organization (OPR continued), family included, concrete organizations (ADEVS, Refuge Bowling Green), two-objective clarity, "kept thinking about the kids" (moral imperative)

WEAKNESSES: Less detailed emotional beats, "after some incidents" underplays trauma, sign language teaching not fully explored, ADEVS founding process compressed

WHY IT WORKS: Teacher identity central, community service mission, 19-year perspective, practical solutions, international scope, cultural adaptation work, children's advocacy, partnership model, full life arc

USAGE NOTES: Preserve 19 years (extraordinary duration), sign language (unique skill), "kept thinking about the kids" (moral imperative), OPR continuation (peace mission), two objectives (clear structure), Refuge Bowling Green partnership (collaboration model), discipline methods education (cultural bridge)
